title: Marsyas
content:
آ آ آ آ CALLICLES (from below)


آ آ آ آ As the sky-brightening south-wind clears the day,
آ آ آ آ And makes the massï؟½d clouds roll,
آ آ آ آ The music of the lyre blows away
آ آ آ آ The clouds that wrap the soul.

آ آ آ آ Oh, that Fate had let me see
آ آ آ آ That triumph of the sweet persuasive lyre!
آ آ آ آ That famous, final victory
آ آ آ آ When jealous Pan with Marsyas did conspire!

آ آ آ آ When, from far Parnassusï؟½ side,
آ آ آ آ Young Apollo, all the pride
آ آ آ آ Of the Phrygian flutes to tame,
آ آ آ آ To the Phrygian highlands came!
آ آ آ آ Where the long green reed-beds sway
آ آ آ آ In the rippled waters grey
آ آ آ آ Of that solitary lake
آ آ آ آ Where Maeanderï؟½s springs are born;
آ آ آ آ Where the ridgï؟½d pine-wooded roots
آ آ آ آ Of Messogis westward break,
آ آ آ آ Mounting westward, high and higher.
آ آ آ آ There was held the famous strife;
آ آ آ آ There the Phrygian brought his flutes,
آ آ آ آ And Apollo brought his lyre;
آ آ آ آ And, when now the westering sun
آ آ آ آ Touchï؟½d the hills, the strife was done,
آ آ آ آ And the attentive Muses said
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Marsyas! thou art vanquishï؟½d.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Then Apolloï؟½s minister
آ آ آ آ Hangï؟½d upon a branching fir
آ آ آ آ Marsyas, that unhappy Faun,
آ آ آ آ And began to whet his knife.
آ آ آ آ But the Maenads, who were there,
آ آ آ آ Left their friend, and with robes flowing
آ آ آ آ In the wind, and loose dark hair
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er their polishï؟½d bosoms blowing,
آ آ آ آ Each her ribbonï؟½d tambourine
آ آ آ آ Flinging on the mountain sod,
آ آ آ آ With a lovely frightenï؟½d mien
آ آ آ آ Came about the youthful God.
آ آ آ آ But he turnï؟½d his beauteous face
آ آ آ آ Haughtily another way,
آ آ آ آ From the grassy sun-warmï؟½d place,
آ آ آ آ Where in proud repose he lay,
آ آ آ آ With one arm over his head,
آ آ آ آ Watching how the whetting sped.

آ آ آ آ But aloof on the lake strand,
آ آ آ آ Did the young Olympus stand,
آ آ آ آ Weeping at his masterï؟½s end;
آ آ آ آ For the Faun had been his friend.
آ آ آ آ For he taught him how to sing.
آ آ آ آ And he taught him flute-playing.
آ آ آ آ Many a morning had they gone
آ آ آ آ To the glimmering mountain lakes,
آ آ آ آ And had torn up by the roots
آ آ آ آ The tall crested water-reeds
آ آ آ آ With long plumes, and soft brown seeds,
آ آ آ آ And had carved them into flutes,
آ آ آ آ Sitting on a tabled stone
آ آ آ آ Where the shoreward ripple breaks.
آ آ آ آ And he taught him how to please
آ آ آ آ The red-snooded Phrygian girls,
آ آ آ آ Whom the summer evening sees
آ آ آ آ Flashing in the danceï؟½s whirls
آ آ آ آ Underneath the starlit trees
آ آ آ آ In the mountain villages.
آ آ آ آ Therefore now Olympus stands,
آ آ آ آ At his masterï؟½s piteous cries
آ آ آ آ Pressing fast with both his hands
آ آ آ آ His white garment to his eyes,
آ آ آ آ Not to see Apolloï؟½s scorn;
آ آ آ آ Ah, poor Faun, poor Faun! ah, poor Faun!
meaning:
About: Matthew Arnold (24 December 1822 ï؟½ 15 April 1888) was an English poet and cultural critic, who worked as an inspector of schools. 
He was the son of Thomas Arnold, the famed headmaster of Rugby School, and brother to Tom Arnold, literary professor, and William Delafield Arnold, novelist and colonial administrator.
